1: The high priest Eliashib begins with the Sheep Gate at the temple’s northeast corner — the priestly section of the wall starts at the sacred center and works outward. The Sheep Gate (šaʿar haṣṣōʾn) was the entry through which animals were brought for sacrifice; its position at the head of the list signals that the restoration of the temple’s functional perimeter is the project’s primary purpose. The Tower of Hananel and the Tower of the Hundred flank the northern wall section adjacent to the temple compound.
2: Men of Jericho working on Jerusalem’s wall — Jericho is 15 miles northeast of Jerusalem. Their participation demonstrates that the wall project mobilized communities from across the region, not just Jerusalem’s residents. The builder list preserves names of ordinary community members who would have left no other historical trace.
3: The Fish Gate (šaʿar haddāḡîm) on the north wall was where fish from the Mediterranean and the Jordan were sold (Zeph 1:10); Tyrian merchants traded fish there (Neh 13:16). The technical details — beams, doors, bolts, bars — reflect full gate reconstruction rather than mere patching; the gate’s strategic position as a primary commercial entry made it a priority.
4: Meremoth son of Uriah appears in Ezra 8:33 as the priest who received and weighed Ezra’s silver and gold — a position of significant trust. Meshullam son of Berechiah’s daughter was married to Tobiah the enemy (Neh 6:18), creating a family connection between a wall-builder and the primary opponent — the community was not divided into neat categories of loyalists and enemies.
5: The Tekoite nobles who “would not put their shoulders to the work of their supervisors” — this is the only negative notation in the entire builder list. Tekoa was approximately 10 miles south of Jerusalem; its town workers participate (and double-section in v.27) while the noble class refuses. This social stratification — artisans and tradespeople rebuilding while the landed gentry exempt themselves — is precisely the class tension Nehemiah confronts in ch5’s debt-crisis. The honest recording of elite refusal in an official document is unusual.
6: The Jeshanah Gate (“old gate,” šaʿar hayyᵉšānāh) on the northwest wall — the reconstruction of the oldest gate is entrusted to two men, Joiada and Meshullam. Full gate reconstruction required laying foundation beams, hanging doors, installing bolts and bars; the paired builders suggest a team effort.
7: Gibeon and Mizpah are towns technically under the governor of Trans-Euphrates rather than the governor of Judah; their participation signals that the wall project extended mobilization beyond Judah’s formal administrative boundaries. The phrase “up to the official residence of the governor of Trans-Euphrates” suggests this wall section abutted the Persian administrative compound — a politically sensitive location.
8: A goldsmith (ṣôrēp̄) and a perfumer (raqqāḥ) building walls — craft-guild workers participating in military infrastructure is a social cross-over. The Broad Wall (haḥômāh hārᵉḥāḇāh) was a substantial older fortification; archaeological excavations in the Jewish Quarter of modern Jerusalem have uncovered a wall section 23 feet wide that may be this structure, built by Hezekiah (Isa 22:10).
9: Rephaiah son of Hur, “ruler of a half-district of Jerusalem” (śar ḥᵃṣî pelek Yᵉrûšālayim) — the pelek system organized Judah into administrative districts (also in vv.12, 14-18); each pelek had a śar (ruler/official). The district administrators are prominently represented in the builder list, suggesting the project was organized partly through the existing administrative structure.
10: Jedaiah repairs the section “opposite his own house” — this residential assignment pattern appears frequently in ch3 (vv.10, 23, 28-30). Assigning builders to the section facing their own home was both practical (they were already there) and motivational (they were defending their own property). The assignment strategy combined communal obligation with personal interest.
11: The Tower of the Ovens (migdal hattannûrîm) — likely near the bakers’ quarter of the city (cf. Jer 37:21, the “court of the guard” near “the bakers’ street”). The tower provides a navigational anchor for the wall’s western section; its name suggests the area where bread was commercially produced for Jerusalem’s population.
12: Shallum and his daughters (bᵉnôṯāyw) — women participating in wall construction. This is the only explicit mention of women working on the wall; the daughters’ inclusion signals either that the labor shortage was severe enough to require all available workers, or that Shallum’s household participated collectively.
13: The Valley Gate (šaʿar haggayʾ) and a thousand cubits of wall (approximately 500 meters) to the Dung Gate — this is the section Nehemiah surveyed at night (2:13-15). Hanun and the residents of Zanoah take responsibility for the longest single section in the list; Zanoah was a town in the Shephelah whose community collectively relocates to work on the distant gate section.
14: The Dung Gate (šaʿar hāʾašpôṯ) — the waste gate on the south side, used to remove refuse, ash, and dead animals from Jerusalem. That a district ruler takes personal responsibility for the least glamorous gate in the city reflects the comprehensive social participation the project required.
15: The Fountain Gate (šaʿar hāʿayin) and the Pool of Siloam — Shallun repairs and roofs (yiqqāhēhû) this gate, the only gate described as being roofed, suggesting it was a significant commercial entry point. The Pool of Siloam was the terminus of Hezekiah’s tunnel (2 Chr 32:30; Isa 22:11), the primary water supply for Jerusalem in siege conditions.
16: “The tombs of David” (qiḇrê ḏāwîḏ) — the Davidic royal tombs on the southeastern slope. Their presence in the repair zone confirms that this wall section skirted the oldest royal burial ground. The “house of the warriors” (bêṯ haggibbōrîm) may refer to a barracks or armory associated with the royal guard.
17: Levites in the builder list — Rehum son of Bani leads the Levites’ section. The Levitical participation in wall-building is noteworthy: the Levites’ primary role was cultic (temple service), not civic construction; their inclusion reflects the community’s pragmatic mobilization of all institutional resources for the task.
18: Bavvai son of Henadad — the Henadad family is a Levitical clan that recurs in the return records (Ezra 2:40; Neh 7:43) and the covenant-sealing list (Neh 10:9). That a single Levitical clan contributes two separate builders for consecutive sections suggests the Henadad clan took coordinated responsibility for a long wall stretch.
19: The armory at the corner (neśeq hammiqṣôaʿ) — a weapons storage facility at one of the wall’s angular projections. The builder list records the proximity of this section to the armory as a navigational reference; the armory’s presence within the rebuilt walls meant that the community had also prepared for ongoing defense.
20: Baruch son of Zabbai “zealously” (beḥārāh, lit. “with heat/ardor”) repaired his section — the only motivational qualifier in the entire builder list. The Hebrew word ḥārāh (to be hot, to burn) describes the intensity of emotional investment that distinguishes his work; this detail would have honored Baruch and his descendants in the community’s memory.
21: Meremoth son of Uriah makes repairs on a second section (he also worked in v.4) — one of two builders in the list who take double sections (the Tekoites also work twice, v.5 and v.27, which may partially redeem the nobles’ refusal). The double contribution is implicitly noteworthy alongside the Tekoite nobles’ failure.
22: Priests from the surrounding region — these priests repair the section near the high priest’s house (v.20-21), continuing the pattern of the priestly class taking responsibility for sections adjacent to sacred and residential areas near the temple. The “surrounding region” (kîḵār) may refer to the central highlands around Jerusalem.
23: Benjamin and Hasshub repair “opposite their house” — the residential assignment continues. Azariah similarly repairs “beside his own house” — the overlap of civic obligation and personal property protection is a consistent organizational principle in the builder list.
24: “From the house of Azariah to the angle and the corner” — precise geographic description using the wall’s physical features (the angle = an interior corner; the corner = an exterior projection) as reference points. The technical precision of the builder list’s geographic references suggests it was compiled from actual surveying records, giving it the character of an official building report.
25: The upper house of the king — a royal residence or administrative building associated with the Persian-era governor. The court of the guard (ḥᵃṣar hammaṭṭārāh) was the prison yard where Jeremiah had been confined during the siege (Jer 37:21); its presence in the builder list confirms the continuity of Jerusalem’s administrative geography from the pre-exilic to the restoration period.
26: The temple servants (nᵉṯînîm) on the Ophel repair up to the Water Gate — the southeastern section of the wall adjacent to the temple mount. The Ophel (“the mound”) was a ridge south of the temple; the nᵉṯînîm’s residential proximity to the section they repair follows the same organizational logic as the priests’ residential assignments.
27: The Tekoites’ second section — the same community whose nobles refused (v.5) contributes its workers a second time for an additional section opposite the great projecting tower. The double contribution by Tekoah’s workers partly redeems the collective honor of their town, despite the nobles’ exemption; the community’s ordinary members compensate for their leaders’ failure.
28: Priests above the Horse Gate (šaʿar hassûsîm) each repair the section “opposite his own house” — the horse gate was the entry to the king’s stables area on the eastern slope (2 Kgs 11:16; Jer 31:40). The residential assignment for priests in this section confirms that priestly housing was distributed across the city, not concentrated only near the temple.
29: Shemaiah son of Shecaniah is “keeper of the East Gate” (šōmēr šaʿar hamizrāḥ) — a hereditary or appointed gatekeeper position; his responsibility for the gate he also rebuilds represents the longest institutional connection in the builder list: a gatekeeper rebuilds the gate he guards.
30: Hananiah son of Shelemiah and Hanun (the sixth son) — the specific ordinal (sixth son) distinguishes this Hanun from the first one (v.13). Meshullam son of Berechiah repairs “opposite his room” (niškaṯô, a chamber or storeroom) — a smaller dwelling unit than a full house, suggesting modest social standing.
31: Malchijah the goldsmith continues the craft-guild worker presence in the builder list (cf. Uzziel the goldsmith in v.8). The Inspection Gate (šaʿar hammiṣpāḏ) may refer to a review gate or a gate where goods were inspected; its proximity to the house of the temple servants and merchants marks the commercial-administrative zone of this section.
32: Between the corner room and the Sheep Gate — the circuit completes at the same gate where it began (v.1). The goldsmiths and merchants close the circle: the same craft guild workers who began the Broad Wall section (v.8) complete the final section, while the priests consecrated the Sheep Gate at the start. The builder list’s narrative frame — priests beginning, merchants ending, with all social categories in between — represents the entire Jerusalem community united in the task.
